raw_case:
A case of the Abernethy malformation: An exceptionally rare clinical entity
I review a case of a 30-year-old woman who presented with diarrhea, nausea and vomiting for several days. She was ultimately diagnosed with acute gastroenteritis but was incidentally found to have a congenital extrahepatic portosystemic shunt, also known as the Abernethy malformation. The Abernethy malformation, first described by Dr John Abernethy in the year 1793, is an exceptionally rare clinical entity.

Introduction

The congenital extrahepatic portosystemic shunt (CEPS), termed the Abernethy Malformation, was first described by Dr. John Abernethy in the year 1793 as he performed a postmortem examination on a 10-monthold girl who died of an unknown cause [1]. In patients with the Abernethy malformation, the splanchnic veins drain directly into the systemic vasculature, often via the inferior vena cava. The underlying cause is thought to be secondary to aberrant embryological development of the peri-intestine venous loop, ultimately resulting in an absent or hypoplastic portal vein with abnormal mesenteric-caval communication [2]. The clinical presentation is variable. Although some patients present in the neonatal period with manifestations of hepatic dysfunction, many remain asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic into adulthood.

Case summary

A 30-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with diarrhea, nausea and vomiting for several days. She also described multiple prior episodes of mental fogging or confusion. She reported no chronic medical conditions, however, as a child required median sternotomy due to congenital heart disease. The specific cardiac anomaly requiring repair was unknown by the patient. She was on no medications. She reported a family history of hypertension and diabetes.She had a blood pressure of 103/65 mmHg. Her heart rate was mildly elevated at 112 beats per minute. She had a normal respiratory rate and oxygen saturation. On physical examination, she had dry mucous membranes. She had a normal cardiopulmonary exam. Her bowel sounds were hyperactive, but the abdomen was nontender. Her laboratory studies, including complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel and urinalysis, were normal. A serum ammonia level was not checked by the clinical team.A computed tomography (CT) scan of the abdomen and pelvis was obtained. This showed an absence of the portal vein with the splenic vein and superior mesenteric veins combining into a singular trunk which joined the inferior vena cava, therefore bypassing the liver entirely (Fig. 1). The entirety of the hepatic vascular supply arose from the hepatic artery. The hepatic parenchyma was heterogeneous, which was thought to be secondary to the above-described vascular anomalies. The patient was diagnosed with viral gastroenteritis and discharged from the emergency department. Prior to discharge, she was instructed to follow up with hepatology as an outpatient.She was seen by hepatology, at which time contrast-enhanced magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the abdomen was also completed. In addition to the congenital portosystemic shunt, the MRI showed a 2.5 cm lesion within the left hepatic lobe most compatible with a hepatic adenoma (Fig. 2). There were numerous additional lesions that were subsequently characterized as focal nodular hyperplasia on a subsequent MRI with Eovist contrast (Fig. 3). The patient's described episodes of confusion were thought to represent hepatic encephalopathy. Therefore, she was started on lactulose. Given the high coexistence of congenital heart disease in patients with the Abernethy malformation, she was referred to cardiology. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a normal heart in both structure and function.

Discussion

The Abernathy malformation can be divided into two subtypes. In Type 1, there is complete diversion of blood from the splanchnic system into the systemic venous system with absence of the portal vein. The Type 1 cases are further subclassified as either Type 1a or Type 1b. In patients characterized as Type 1a, the splenic and superior mesenteric veins drain separately into the inferior vena cava, renal vein or iliac vein. On the contrary, in Type 1b the superior mesenteric vein and splenic vein join together to form a common trunk that drains into a systemic vein, typically the inferior vena cava or the right atrium. Type 2 shunts are characterized by an intact, though hypoplastic, intrahepatic portal venous system with partial systemic shunting of portal blood through a side-to-side extrahepatic communication [3].The clinical presentation of CEPS is variable. Patients may present in the neonatal period with growth restriction, cholestasis and hepatic encephalopathy; others may remain asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic into adulthood [[4], [5], [6]]. Many patients are diagnosed after being evaluated for other developmental abnormalities, since there is a high coincidence of comorbid processes such as congenital heart disease [1]. Biliary atresia, situs inversus, polysplenia and malrotation are other frequently reported associations [7].With portosystemic shunting of blood, there is deranged metabolism of many substances, such as ammonia, leading to various clinical manifestations including hepatic encephalopathy [2]. The liver in patients with CEPS undergoes regenerative nodular hyperplasia as a response to absent or altered portal venous flow, which ultimately may progress to the development of hepatic neoplasms such as hepatocellular carcinoma, hepatoblastoma, and hepatic adenoma [8]. The development of hepatopulmonary syndrome has been reported in some patients [2].Patients with CEPS can be diagnosed noninvasively though either ultrasound, CT or MRI. Treatment of such patients depends on the underlying type of shunting, either complete (Type 1) or partial (Type 2). In patients with Type 1 shunting, intervention with occlusion or embolization of the shunt is not performed, as the shunt is the only pathway of blood flow for the splanchnic vessels. Clinical, biochemical and imaging follow-up with medical management is the only treatment option unless liver transplantation is possible [2]. In patients with Type 2 shunting, however, there is a role for either surgical or transcatheter shunt occlusion with or without surgical reconstruction of the portal vein [9,10].

Conclusion

The Abernethy malformation is an exceptionally rare clinical entity with fewer than 200 cases reported in the literature to date [11]. Patients may present in the neonatal period or remain asymptomatic to minimally symptomatic into adulthood. Treatment is variable and depends on the degree of portosystemic shunting, with medical management and eventual liver transplantation serving as the only options for patients with Type 1 disease. A coordinated approach with multiple subspecialists is necessary in the management of such patients given the high incidence of comorbid congenital anomalies affecting other organ systems.

Patient consent

Written consent for publication was obtained from the patient. Approval to publish this manuscript has been given by my institution, its legal representative and an ethics committee. Publication of this document is in accordance with the local legislation.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 30-year-old woman.
- **Chief Complaint:** Diarrhea, nausea, and vomiting for several days.
- **History of Present Illness:** Multiple prior episodes of mental fogging or confusion.
- **Past Medical History:** Congenital heart disease requiring median sternotomy during childhood; specifics unknown.
- **Personal History:** No medications reported.
- **Family History:** Hypertension and diabetes.
- **Physical Examination:** Blood pressure: 103/65 mmHg; heart rate: 112 bpm; normal respiratory rate and oxygen saturation. Dry mucous membranes, hyperactive bowel sounds, nontender abdomen, and normal cardiopulmonary exam.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
    - Laboratory studies (CBC, CMP, urinalysis): Normal.
    - Abdominal and pelvic CT: Absence of the portal vein; splenic and superior mesenteric veins joining into a trunk bypassing the liver and draining into the IVC. Heterogeneous hepatic parenchyma.
    - Contrast-enhanced MRI: 2.5 cm hepatic adenoma and additional lesions identified as focal nodular hyperplasia (MRI with Eovist contrast).
    - Transthoracic echocardiography: Normal heart structure and function.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Evaluation of gastrointestinal symptoms:** Initial diagnosis of acute gastroenteritis aligns with diarrhea, nausea, and vomiting for several days. No evidence of structural or functional abnormalities related directly to gut pathology during the workup.
2. **Assessment of repeated confusion (mental fogging):** Likely represents hepatic encephalopathy given the elevated likelihood of ammonia accumulation in the Abernethy malformation. Serum ammonia levels, though not checked, strengthen suspicion in the context of portosystemic shunting.
3. **Characterization of vascular and hepatic anomalies:** CT and MRI findings, including absent portal vein and hepatic lesions (adenoma and focal nodular hyperplasia), confirmed the congenital extrahepatic portosystemic shunt (Abernethy malformation). Features align with Type 1b classification due to common trunk drainage of the mesenteric and splenic veins into the IVC.
4. **Congenital anomaly associations:** History of congenital heart disease with normal echocardiography suggests historical resolution or unrelated anomaly. High prevalence of other associated systemic abnormalities (e.g., situs inversus, polysplenia) is considered but ruled out based on clinical and imaging findings.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Abernethy malformation (congenital extrahepatic portosystemic shunt, Type 1b) with secondary hepatic encephalopathy, hepatic adenoma, and focal nodular hyperplasia.

results.input:
Please carefully study the following patient case summary, conduct a comprehensive and in-depth diagnostic analysis, and clearly provide the final diagnosis result.

- **Patient Information:** 30-year-old woman.
- **Chief Complaint:** Diarrhea, nausea, and vomiting for several days.
- **History of Present Illness:** Multiple prior episodes of mental fogging or confusion.
- **Past Medical History:** Congenital heart disease requiring median sternotomy during childhood; specifics unknown.
- **Personal History:** No medications reported.
- **Family History:** Hypertension and diabetes.
- **Physical Examination:** Blood pressure: 103/65 mmHg; heart rate: 112 bpm; normal respiratory rate and oxygen saturation. Dry mucous membranes, hyperactive bowel sounds, nontender abdomen, and normal cardiopulmonary exam.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
    - Laboratory studies (CBC, CMP, urinalysis): Normal.
    - Abdominal and pelvic CT: Absence of the portal vein; splenic and superior mesenteric veins joining into a trunk bypassing the liver and draining into the IVC. Heterogeneous hepatic parenchyma.
    - Contrast-enhanced MRI: 2.5 cm hepatic adenoma and additional lesions identified as focal nodular hyperplasia (MRI with Eovist contrast).
    - Transthoracic echocardiography: Normal heart structure and function.

---


results.content:
<think>
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's chief complaint: diarrhea, nausea, and vomiting for several days. She also has a history of mental fogging or confusion. Her past medical history includes congenital heart disease with a median sternotomy as a child, but specifics aren't known. Family history mentions hypertension and diabetes. 

Physical exam shows low blood pressure (103/65), tachycardia (112 bpm), dry mucous membranes, hyperactive bowel sounds, and no abdominal tenderness. The lab tests are normal, which is interesting because she's having GI symptoms. The abdominal and pelvic CT shows absence of the portal vein, with splenic and superior mesenteric veins joining into a trunk bypassing the liver and draining into the IVC. Also, the liver has heterogeneous parenchyma. The MRI found a hepatic adenoma and focal nodular hyperplasia. Echocardiogram is normal.

First, the absence of the portal vein suggests a congenital anomaly. The portal vein usually carries blood from the intestines to the liver. If it's absent, the blood from the intestines would bypass the liver, which is called portosystemic shunting. This can lead to hepatic encephalopathy, which might explain her episodes of mental fogging or confusion. The heterogeneous liver parenchyma might indicate underlying liver disease, maybe cirrhosis or other chronic conditions. The presence of hepatic adenoma and focal nodular hyperplasia are benign liver lesions but could be associated with certain conditions like chronic liver disease or metabolic issues.

The GI symptoms (diarrhea, nausea, vomiting) might be related to the portosystemic shunting. Bypassing the liver could lead to malabsorption or altered gut flora, contributing to diarrhea. Also, hepatic encephalopathy can sometimes present with GI symptoms, though more commonly with confusion. The normal labs are a bit confusing because you'd expect some derangements with liver issues, but maybe her symptoms are not yet severe enough, or the labs are only mildly abnormal.

Her past surgery (median sternotomy) might be related to her congenital heart disease. However, the echocardiogram is normal, so maybe the heart issue is resolved. The family history of hypertension and diabetes might be a red herring here, unless there's a genetic component to her liver condition.

Putting it all together, the key findings are the portosystemic shunt (absent portal vein with collateral circulation) and the liver lesions. The most likely diagnosis is a congenital portosystemic shunt, which can lead to hepatic encephalopathy (explaining the mental fog) and possibly GI symptoms due to altered metabolism or absorption. The hepatic adenoma and focal nodular hyperplasia are secondary findings that might be associated with chronic liver disease or a genetic condition like hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT), which can cause vascular malformations. However, the presence of a portosystemic shunt is more specific here. So, the primary diagnosis is congenital portosystemic shunt with hepatic encephalopathy and associated liver lesions.
</think>

```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with acute gastrointestinal symptoms (diarrhea, nausea, vomiting) and a history of intermittent mental confusion. The physical exam reveals signs of volume depletion (dry mucous membranes) and tachycardia, but no abdominal tenderness. Laboratory tests are unremarkable, which may suggest a non-inflammatory or non-infectious etiology. 

<step 2> The abdominal CT reveals an absent portal vein with collateral circulation (splenic and superior mesenteric veins draining directly into the IVC), indicating a congenital portosystemic shunt. This anatomical abnormality bypasses the liver, leading to systemic absorption of intestinal toxins and metabolic byproducts, which can cause hepatic encephalopathy (explaining the mental fogging/confusion) and potentially contribute to gastrointestinal dysfunction.

<step 3> The MRI identifies hepatic adenoma and focal nodular hyperplasia, which are benign liver lesions often associated with chronic liver disease, hormonal influences, or genetic conditions (e.g., HHT). These findings, combined with heterogeneous liver parenchyma, suggest underlying liver dysfunction or cirrhosis, possibly secondary to long-term portosystemic shunting.

<step 4> The patient’s congenital heart disease history and median sternotomy may be unrelated to her current presentation, as the echocardiogram is normal. Family history of hypertension and diabetes does not directly explain the findings but may contribute to comorbidities.

<step 5> The acute GI symptoms could be exacerbated by hepatic encephalopathy, altered gut microbiota, or metabolic disturbances due to the portosystemic shunt. The absence of lab abnormalities may reflect early or compensated disease.

### Answer:
Congenital portosystemic shunt with hepatic encephalopathy and associated hepatic lesions (adenoma and focal nodular hyperplasia).
```
